name: aws-penetration-testing
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "pentest AWS", "test AWS security", "enumerate IAM", "exploit cloud infrastructure", "AWS privilege escalation", "S3 bucket testing", "metadata SSRF", "Lambda exploitation", or needs guidance on Amazon Web Services security assessment.
capabilities: Enumerate IAM users, roles, groups, and attached policies using AWS CLI
Test SSRF vulnerabilities against AWS Instance Metadata Service (IMDSv1 and IMDSv2)
Assess S3 bucket configurations for public access and misconfigurations
Analyze Lambda functions for code injection and privilege escalation paths
Document credential exposure risks in EBS volumes and container environments
Generate security audit findings with remediation recommendations
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 287 patterns across 2 files (881 lines). Most findings are FALSE POSITIVEs because files contain Markdown documentation (not executable code). However, content includes sensitive offensive security techniques (SSRF exploitation, privilege escalation, persistence mechanisms) requiring explicit authorization warnings. Recommend: publish with prominent authorization disclaimers and user acknowledgment requirements.
valueStatement: Organizations need to validate their AWS cloud security posture against real-world attack techniques. This skill provides authorized security teams with comprehensive methodologies for IAM enumeration, SSRF exploitation testing, S3 bucket assessment, and privilege escalation detection.
userTitle: Perform AWS Penetration Testing and Security Assessment
limitations: Requires valid AWS credentials and explicit written authorization before use
Cannot execute AWS CLI commands directly - provides guidance only
Not intended for production environments without change approval process
Does not include automated scanning - manual testing methodology required

useCases.targetUser: Security consultants and red team operators
useCases.title: Authorized Red Team Engagement
useCases.description: Security consultants performing authorized penetration testing against client AWS environments to identify misconfigurations and privilege escalation paths before malicious actors exploit them.

useCases.title: Cloud Security Audit
useCases.description: Internal security teams assessing their organization's AWS security posture against known attack techniques to validate defensive controls and monitoring capabilities.

useCases.targetUser: Security researchers and cloud security students
useCases.title: Security Research and Training
useCases.description: Security researchers and students learning AWS attack techniques in controlled lab environments (AWSGoat, CloudGoat) to improve defensive security skills.

promptTemplates.title: Basic IAM Enumeration
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me enumerate IAM permissions for the current AWS identity. I have authorized access and need to document what permissions this identity has. Start with sts get-caller-identity and show me how to list attached policies.
promptTemplates.scenario: Initial reconnaissance during authorized security assessment

promptTemplates.title: S3 Bucket Security Assessment
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to assess S3 bucket configurations for public access vulnerabilities. Show me the AWS CLI commands to list buckets, check bucket policies, and identify publicly accessible objects in our authorized test environment.

promptTemplates.title: SSRF to Metadata Testing
promptTemplates.prompt: We're testing our web application for SSRF vulnerabilities that could access AWS metadata endpoints. Document the IMDSv1 and IMDSv2 techniques so we can verify our instance metadata protection controls are working.

promptTemplates.title: Privilege Escalation Path Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze the IAM permissions we've enumerated and identify potential privilege escalation paths. Check for dangerous permissions like iam:CreateAccessKey, iam:AttachUserPolicy, and lambda:UpdateFunctionCode that could lead to admin access.

bestPractices: Always obtain written authorization documenting scope, systems, and testing window before beginning any penetration testing activities
Enable CloudTrail logging before testing begins and preserve all logs for post-engagement analysis and client reporting
Use dedicated test credentials and avoid testing against production environments without explicit change approval and rollback procedures
antiPatterns: Never test AWS resources outside the authorized scope - unauthorized access violates computer crime laws even with good intentions
Do not disable security controls (CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Security Hub) permanently - temporary bypasses must be documented and restored
Avoid leaving persistent backdoors or access mechanisms after engagement completion - all test artifacts must be removed during cleanup

faq.question: What authorization do I need before using this skill?
faq.answer: You must have written authorization from the AWS account owner documenting: specific accounts/resources in scope, testing time window, approved techniques, and emergency contact procedures. Unauthorized testing violates AWS Acceptable Use Policy and computer crime laws.

faq.question: Can this skill execute AWS commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides guidance and documentation for AWS CLI commands. You must manually execute commands in your own terminal with your own credentials. The skill cannot access your AWS account directly.

faq.question: Is it legal to test AWS services I don't own?
faq.answer: No. You may only test AWS resources you own or have explicit written authorization to test. AWS prohibits unauthorized penetration testing in their Acceptable Use Policy. Contact AWS Security for their penetration testing policy if testing AWS infrastructure.

faq.question: What tools do I need to use this skill effectively?
faq.answer: Required: AWS CLI configured with credentials, Python 3, and boto3 library. Recommended: Pacu (AWS exploitation framework), Prowler or ScoutSuite (security auditing), enumerate-iam (permission enumeration). All tools should only be used in authorized environments.

faq.question: How do I prevent GuardDuty alerts during testing?
faq.answer: GuardDuty will likely generate alerts during penetration testing. This is expected behavior. Coordinate with the security operations team to whitelist test activity or temporarily adjust alerting thresholds. Never disable GuardDuty without authorization.

faq.question: What cleanup is required after testing?
faq.answer: Remove all created resources (IAM users, access keys, Lambda functions, EC2 instances). Delete any backdoored code or persistence mechanisms. Restore modified configurations. Document all changes made for client verification. Preserve testing logs for the final report.
